bacteria.name: E. Coli
bacteria.tierDescription: Harmless in the right place. Dangerous only because it got lost.

tabs.fieldReport:
You have met this one before. You just did not know it. Right now, in the gut, hundreds of billions of E. coli are living peacefully, making vitamin K, asking for nothing. Those ones are fine. Those ones are friends.

This one took a wrong turn somewhere near the intestinal wall and ended up in the bloodstream, where it has absolutely no business being. It knows it. It is panicking. That makes it predictable.

The lipopolysaccharide shell is its only real armor, and your complement system has been punching holes in it since before you arrived. By the time you see it, it is already dying. Your job is just to finish the sentence.
tabs.combatNotes:
Phagocytose ends this fast. Do not overthink it.

The spread attack (TOXIN SPRAY) is a bluff — it fires wide because it is scared, not because it is smart. Stay mobile. This is not the enemy that kills you. This is the enemy that teaches you the controls before the enemy that kills you shows up.

RECOMMENDED: Macrophage. Engulf devours it in two hits.

WATCH FOR: Mutant variants with altered indicator rings. A slightly darker red means something changed.
tabs.funFact:
E. coli can evolve antibiotic resistance and pass that resistance to completely different species of bacteria — not to its children, but to its neighbors, right now, in real time. This is called horizontal gene transfer.

This is why superbugs exist. The bacteria are not getting stronger through generations. They are sharing notes in real time.

Also: E. coli was the first organism used to produce synthetic human insulin, in 1982. The organism behind food poisoning produces the medicine that keeps diabetics alive. Biology contains multitudes.

bacteria2.name: Staph
bacteria2.tierDescription: Annoying. Rarely lethal in a healthy host. Dangerous when the door is left open.

tabs.fieldReport:
One in three people are currently carrying Staphylococcus aureus in their nose. Not sick. Not infected. Just carrying it. It is comfortable there.

Staph is the houseguest who never causes trouble until the day it does — and then it causes catastrophic trouble. It entered through a small cut the host did not think was worth worrying about.

Unlike E. coli, Staph is gram-positive: thicker cell wall, harder to crack. Your complement system slows it down but does not finish it. It can form biofilm when it feels threatened. Do not give it time to settle.
tabs.combatNotes:
Fires faster than E. coli and hits harder. Respect that.

BIOFILM BURST fires from the right side of the grid. Hug the left wall when you see the charge animation — the projectile spread fans rightward.

Macrophage's Engulf is ideal here; the self-heal matters. Do not let it sit in the back column. Some variants develop a damage shield if you are passive. Aggression wins.
tabs.funFact:
Staphylococcus aureus was the bacterium Alexander Fleming noticed killing his cultures in 1928 — the observation that led directly to penicillin.

The bacterium that taught us antibiotics exist is now one of the leading causes of antibiotic-resistant infections on Earth.

Fleming accepted his Nobel Prize in 1945 and explicitly warned that misusing penicillin would cause resistant strains. Nobody listened. MRSA was identified in 1961, one year after methicillin was introduced. He was right within twelve months.

virus.name: Rhinovirus
virus.tierDescription: Cannot kill you. Does not need to. Its strategy is volume and your underestimation.

tabs.fieldReport:
Nobody respects the rhinovirus. That is why it infects a billion people per year.

Over 160 known serotypes, each slightly different enough that antibodies from last winter do not recognize this version. Your memory B-cells are useless here. By the time your adaptive immune system identifies it, the virus has replicated ten million times and moved on.

It thrives at 33 degrees Celsius — the temperature of the nasal passages. The bloodstream is too warm for it. It is here because it took a wrong turn and it is moving fast.
tabs.combatNotes:
Speed fight. Not a power fight.

VIRAL BURST hits five directions simultaneously. There is no safe column, only a safe moment. The attack has a half-second pause before release. Move one row on that pause.

T-Cell handles this most efficiently. Do NOT use Macrophage — too slow, too much chip damage.
tabs.funFact:
You have heard your whole life that cold weather causes colds. It does not.

Cold weather causes people to go inside, share recycled air, and touch the same surfaces. The virus loves this. The cold is innocent. Blame the heating system.

Rhinovirus evolved to prefer 33 degrees because that is the temperature inside human noses. In a very specific sense, your nose is its natural habitat and you are an inconvenient obstacle.

virus2.name: Influenza
virus2.tierDescription: Seasonal nuisance in good years. Civilization-ending in bad ones.

tabs.fieldReport:
Influenza has an eight-segment RNA genome, and those segments can shuffle with bird flu and swine flu segments when multiple strains infect the same cell simultaneously. This is called antigenic shift. This is how pandemic strains are born.

The 1918 pandemic killed 50 to 100 million people. More than World War One, in a fraction of the time. Every time this virus replicates, it makes errors. Every error is a lottery ticket. Most losing. Some winning. You are the only thing standing between this host and whatever the lottery produces.
tabs.combatNotes:
MUTATION WAVE: five projectiles spreading upward. Orange shimmer is the tell. Move to the bottom row when you see it. Stay there. The spread climbs.

NK Cell's Granzyme bypasses surface protein recognition — true damage hits even variants your memory B-cells cannot identify.

B-Cell Memory Tag works on standard encounters. Does NOT work on antigenic shift variants. That is biologically intentional.
tabs.funFact:
The 1918 flu killed young, healthy adults at higher rates than children or the elderly.

The hypothesis: strong immune systems mounted a more violent inflammatory response. A cytokine storm. The immune system trying to destroy the virus destroyed the lungs in the process.

In the worst pandemic in modern history, having a robust immune system made you statistically more likely to die. Sometimes the weapon is also the wound.

superbug.name: MRSA
superbug.tierDescription: Resistant strains. Few treatment options. Kills healthy adults. No shortcuts.

tabs.fieldReport:
Fourteen antibiotics. That is how many antimicrobial agents have been deployed against Staphylococcus aureus since penicillin in the 1940s. MRSA has outlasted every single one.

Not because it is intelligent. Because bacteria reproduce in twenty minutes and evolution is just statistics. When you expose billions of organisms to a poison and a handful survive by chance, those survivors become the new normal within days.

We did this. Agricultural antibiotic overuse. Patients not finishing courses. Antibiotics in hand soap. We trained this organism to survive everything we could throw at it. It kills 35,000 people in the United States alone every year. Your complement system cannot touch it. There is no antibiotic behind you.
tabs.combatNotes:
THE CORRECT SEQUENCE:
1. Dendritic Cell — Antigen Scan. Strips 40% defense. Not optional.
2. Morph to NK Cell (Granzyme) or T-Cell (Cytotoxic Strike + Apoptosis execute).
3. Finish before the special fires.

ANTIBIOTIC SHIELD SLAM telegraphs for over a second. Slow. Will end you if you are already hurt. Heal before the special window.

Extramedullary Hematopoiesis upgrade pays for itself here.
tabs.funFact:
MRSA evolved partly because we fed antibiotics to healthy livestock to make them grow faster. Billions of animals. Continuous low-level exposure. Decades. A perfect training environment for resistance.

The strains that emerged in farm animals transferred to humans.

The post-antibiotic era is not science fiction. The WHO has a projected timeline. MRSA is not the end of that story. It is the beginning.

parasite.name: Plasmodium
parasite.tierDescription: May have killed more humans than any other organism in history. It shaped your genome.

tabs.fieldReport:
Some scientists believe Plasmodium falciparum has killed more human beings than any other organism in the history of our species. Across all of human history, on every continent the Anopheles mosquito could reach.

Malaria shaped our genome. The sickle cell trait persists in populations with historical malaria exposure because one copy provides partial protection. Evolution decided dying of sickle cell was an acceptable price for surviving malaria. That is how dangerous this organism is.

Plasmodium is not a bacterium or a virus. It is a eukaryotic parasite — genetically closer to you than E. coli is. It hides inside red blood cells until thousands burst simultaneously. This is why malaria fever comes in precise 48-hour cycles.
tabs.combatNotes:
Eosinophil was built for this. MBP DUMP does 3x damage against parasites — this is not game balance, it is biology. Eosinophil's Major Basic Protein is genuinely toxic to parasitic membranes.

Complement cannot reach Plasmodium inside cells. Do not rely on passive damage.

MEROZOITE RELEASE sends tracking projectiles that follow your last position with a delay. Counter: move one row as they release, then hold still. Stop moving. Let them miss.
tabs.funFact:
Artemisinin, the most effective anti-malarial drug, was discovered by Tu Youyou by researching ancient traditional medicine texts.

She found a 1,700-year-old recipe using sweet wormwood, written during the Jin dynasty. She extracted the active compound in 1972. She won the Nobel Prize in 2015.

The answer to one of humanity's oldest killers was in a book from the fourth century, waiting for someone to look.

Artemisinin-partial resistance is now emerging in Southeast Asia. We are watching this happen in real time.

fungus.name: Candida
fungus.tierDescription: Lives inside almost every healthy adult on Earth. Patient. Opportunistic. Waiting.

tabs.fieldReport:
Candida albicans lives inside almost every healthy adult on Earth right now. In the mouth. The gut. The skin. It belongs there — peaceful by default, kept in check by competing bacteria and a functioning immune system.

When antibiotics kill those bacteria, Candida has less competition. When the immune system weakens, Candida has less opposition. When both happen at once, it transforms.

Candida albicans switches between two physical forms: the round, budding yeast form and the hyphal form — long, branching filaments that penetrate tissue and build biofilms on medical devices nearly impossible to sterilize. The trigger for the switch is body temperature. You are exactly warm enough to turn this organism dangerous.
tabs.combatNotes:
HYPHA STORM does not fire in a pattern. It fills space. Aim for corner tiles during the storm — edge positions take fewer hits than center columns.

Neutrophil's NET Trap physically ensnares hyphae — this is accurate. Neutrophil extracellular traps literally capture fungal hyphae in real immune responses.

Complement is effective here. Candida's cell wall beta-glucan activates the lectin pathway.

Kill speed matters. Spore density increases over fight duration. Aggression wins.
tabs.funFact:
Candida auris was first identified in 2009. It is now resistant to all three major classes of antifungal drugs in some strains. It persists on surfaces for weeks. Standard lab equipment frequently misidentifies it as a harmless fungus.

The CDC designated it an Urgent Threat in 2022.

MRSA took forty years to reach that designation. Candida auris did it in thirteen.

You will encounter it in this game. Its indicator ring is white. It does not behave like standard Candida. You have been warned.
